[10] HER3 lacks tyrosine kinase activity but relies on other receptor tyrosine kinases, most notably ERBB , to phosphorylate its tyrosine residue and activate the downstream signaling cascades. HER2-containing heterodimers have the highest mitogenic potential among all HER complexes. Between all HER2 pairs, HER2/HER3 heterodimer is the most potent activator of the PI3K/AKT signaling cascade via binding of the p85 subunit of PI3K to HER3. Document [9] (Title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Mechanism -- Activation of Cytoplasmic Kinase Domains) TKDs of RTKs are comprised of a C-lobe, N-lobe, and "activation loop." For a TKD to become functional, both the "activation loop" and N-lobe alpha-C helix must adopt a specific, "active" configuration. In Ins receptors, a tyrosine on the activation loop occupies the kinase active site, forming a stable configuration that blocks ATP and protein substrate entry.

Document [12] (Title: Cetuximab -- Mechanism of Action) Cetuximab is a recombinant chimeric human/mouse IgG1 monoclonal antibody which binds to epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) and competitively inhibits the binding of epidermal growth factor (EGF) and other ligands. EGFR is a member of the ErbB family of receptors. When inactive, EGFR is a monomer, but when bound by epidermal growth factor or transforming growth factor-alpha (TGF-alpha), it forms homodimers or heterodimers with another member of the ErbB family of receptors. Dimerization activates the intracellular tyrosine kinase region of EGFR, resulting in autophosphorylation and initiating a cascade of intracellular events. The EGFR signaling pathway regulates cell differentiation, proliferation, migration, angiogenesis, and apoptosis, all of which become deregulated in cancer cells. Cetuximab binds to EGFR with high specificity and a higher affinity than either epidermal growth factor or TGF-alpha, thus blocking the ligand-induced phosphorylation of EGFR. Also, cetuximab enhances the effects of irinotecan and radiotherapy in experimental systems. K-ras , a small G-protein downstream of EGFR and a vital component of the EGFR signaling cascade, can acquire activating mutations in exon 2, thus isolating the pathway from the effect of EGFR and rendering EGFR inhibitors ineffective. [5] [2] [4]

Document [15] (Title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Pathophysiology) The fusion oncoprotein BCR-ABL1 defines CML. 90% to 95% of patients with CML have a shortened chromosome 22 because of a reciprocal translocation t(9;22) (q34;q11.2) called the Philadelphia chromosome. The ABL1 gene encodes a non-receptor tyrosine kinase on chromosome 9, and BCR is a breakpoint cluster region on chromosome 22. The translated oncoprotein, in most cases, is 210-kd and called p210 BCR-ABL1 . Alternative splicing results in p190 and p230 BCR-ABL1 , which may show different presentations. This oncoprotein acts as a constitutively expressed defective tyrosine kinase. The downstream pathways affected include JAK/STAT, PI3K/AKT, and RAS/MEK; they involve cell growth, cell survival, inhibition of apoptosis, and activation of transcription factors. [5] The remainder of patients have variant or complex translocations involving additional chromosomes detected by routine cytogenetics or a cryptic BCR-ABL1 t ranslocation detected with fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) or reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain reaction (PCR). [6]

Document [17] (Title: Biochemistry, Hormones -- Mechanism) The next major cell surface receptor is the insulin/IGF receptor, which has intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity. It is composed of alpha and beta chains and exists as either hetero or homodimers depending on how these chains combine. Signal transduction initiates when insulin or IGF binds the IR domain of the receptor, leading to a conformational change that causes autophosphorylation of tyrosine residues. Phosphorylation leads to recruitment of “adapter” proteins, names insulin receptor substrate (IRS) proteins and the SH2-B protein. These recruited proteins induce further downstream signaling of multiple pathways, including the MEK-MAPK pathway [7] . The net effect is the insertion of membranous GLUT4 channels and glucose transport and activation of enzymes that promote glycolysis and anabolism.

Document [19] (Title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Mechanism -- Linking of Activated RTK to Cell Signaling) Except for EGFR and RET, transautophosphorylation provides the means by which RTKs link to cell signaling pathways. Autophosphorylation occurs in two phases. The first phase has been described previously; trans-autophosphorylation of specific tyrosine residues relieves cis-autoinhibition, allowing the TKD to adopt the active configuration and optimizing enzyme kinetics. In second phase autophosphorylation, additional tyrosines in the juxtamembrane region, C-terminal tail, and TKD are phosphorylated, creating phosphotyrosine-binding sites that can be bound by kinases and adaptor proteins possessing SH2 or PTB domains. The TKD can also phosphorylate tyrosines on associated docking proteins, which have no enzymatic activity themselves but provide more potential binding sites. Examples of docking proteins include FRS2, IRS1, and Gab-1. The cell signaling pathways triggered by these interactions are discussed in the Cellular section. [2]

Document [23] (Title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Keratitis -- Introduction) The receptor tyrosine kinases are enzymes that are transmembrane receptors on the cell surface. They typically consist of an extracellular domain that functions to bind to specific ligands. A transmembrane region encompassing the entirety of the cell membrane and the intracellular kinase domain complete the RTK. Once a specific ligand binds to the extracellular portion of the receptor, the protein dimerizes and changes its structure, thus allowing the intracellular kinase domain to catalyze the addition of phosphate groups to the tyrosine residues in itself (autophosphorylation).

REA disruption of the signal transduction, with inhibition of transcription of various proteins, is also observed due to inhibition of the intracellular phosphorylation pathways, including the Raf/MEF/ERK and the PI3K/m TOR/AKT pathways. Sorafenib is administered orally at 400 mg twice daily, with an oral bioavailability of 92 percent. More than 9.5 percent of the drug is protein-bound to albumin and alpha 1 acid glycoprotein. It is mainly metabolized to an N oxide metabolite by the Cytochrome p 450 enzyme system. The elimination half-life of the molecule is between 20 to 39 hours. Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81) Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.
Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.
Document [4] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916) Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.
